description: Provide a reliable web-api for collaborative document editing and search (module: signal-app / app) that supports offline-first sync, indexing, and plugin extensibility.
Keep document snapshots durable and recoverable via SnapshotStore and DiskDocumentSnapshotStore so users can restore state and resume sync sessions.
Maintain accurate presence and session tracking using PeerPresence and PresenceTracker so peers see timely presence updates.
Support summarization and AI features via LocalSummarizer and RemoteSummarizer while allowing fallbacks for offline operation.
Single module boundary 'signal-app' hosts most services (app/*) — the system is organized as a monolithic application boundary containing stores, sync, indexer, and plugins.
Pluggable indexing/search architecture: InvertedIndex, Indexer, WorkerPool, and SearchPlugin form a pipeline for text indexing and query processing.
Sync layer split into SyncEngine and SyncManager with explicit contracts for SyncQueue, SyncSessionTracker, VectorClock, and ConflictResolution to manage offline/online state.
Extensibility through Plugin/PluginHost and ExportPlugin/SearchPlugin contracts; PluginContext mediates plugin interactions with core services (storage, telemetry, events).
LocalSummarizer is exported in app/src/ai/summarizer.ts and its summarize(document) is detected as a pure function (no IO), while RemoteSummarizer.summarize has async/io side effects.

There are duplicated service nodes in the topology: DeprecatedDocumentStore, FileSnapshotStore, SnapshotStore, DocumentSnapshotService, DiskDocumentSnapshotStore, DocumentStore, SyncEngine, SyncManager appear multiple times indicating either parallel implementations or model duplication.

Contracts enumerate many domain types (Document, DocumentSnapshot, DocumentLink, LinkKind, SearchQuery, SearchResult, StorageEvent types), suggesting a well-specified domain model and an event-driven storage bus (StorageEventBus).

An invariant 'error-boundary: Defensive counter decrement' exists ensuring LocalSummarizer.globalActiveRequests is decremented even if release fails—code includes defensive guards for resource accounting.
Presence and session-related contracts (PeerPresence, PresenceTracker, PeerSession, SyncSessionTracker) are present, implying explicit handling of online peers and session lifecycle.

There are many contract-only nodes (84 contracts) such as SearchPlugin, ExportPlugin, TransportSend, OfflineSyncQueue — some contracts may lack concrete implementations or have thin/unstable implementations.
Topology shows Indexer, InvertedIndex, WorkerPool and InvertedIndexSearchHit types; a focused inverted-index search pipeline exists but its runtime scaling characteristics are not captured.
A 'TypeScript adoption' decision node exists; codebase likely mixes strict typing with possible legacy JS/TS interop, which could hide runtime type gaps.
Constraint nodes include 'Authentication required', 'Async job processing', 'Observability', 'Persistent storage', and 'Test coverage required'—non-functional requirements are explicit in the model.
TelemetryEvent and SearchResultSnapshot contracts are present; the system captures telemetry and snapshot-level search results for observability and reproducibility.
Authentication required: All peer-facing actions (presence, sync, plugin access) must be gated by authenticated PeerInfo/TransportSend to prevent unauthorized mutation.
Persistent storage: SnapshotStore/DiskDocumentSnapshotStore must persist a consistent sequence of DocumentSnapshot and VersionHistory updates to avoid data loss during sync.
Async job processing: WorkerPool and Indexer must process indexing jobs in bounded time and surface backpressure to SyncManager to avoid unbounded memory growth.
Observability: TelemetryEvent and StorageEventBus must capture enough signals (index stats, sync ack/failures, conflict records) to detect and triage divergence between peers.
Test coverage required: Key contracts (SyncEngine, ConflictResolution, OfflineSyncQueue, PresenceTracker) must be covered by automated tests to prevent regressions in core distributed behaviors.

Race conditions will surface under concurrent sync and indexing: SyncEngine/SyncManager interacting with Indexer/WorkerPool may produce inconsistent index states when concurrent writes and snapshotting occur.
Legacy/deprecated stores (DeprecatedDocumentStore) coexistence may cause behavioral inconsistencies—some code paths may still write to deprecated stores leading to lost or divergent snapshots.
RemoteSummarizer usage will cause tail latency spikes and retries if network IO is not rate-limited; LocalSummarizer alone may be insufficient for long or complex documents.
PluginHost exposes surface area for misbehaving plugins: a buggy SearchPlugin or ExportPlugin could block WorkerPool threads or leak resources if PluginContext lacks strict quotas.
Partial TypeScript adoption will let subtle runtime type errors slip through at boundaries (e.g., StorageEvent handling or TransportSend payloads), causing uncaught exceptions in production flows.

Intent: offline-first syncing (OfflineSyncQueue, SyncQueue). Reality: presence of RemoteSummarizer and explicit Remote IO suggests dependence on network services, creating a tension between offline capability and remote features.

Intent: modular plugin system (Plugin, PluginHost). Reality: single module 'signal-app' monolith and many contract-only nodes indicates plugins may be poorly isolated or under-implemented, risking core stability.

Intent: evolve APIs (contracts for many domain types). Reality: duplicated legacy services (DeprecatedDocumentStore alongside new stores) create friction in migration, increasing complexity and potential for inconsistencies.

Intent: strong observability and defensive invariants (error-boundary, telemetry). Reality: inferred gaps in test coverage and duplicated service nodes increase the risk that invariants are not enforced under complex failure modes.
